# /optimize skill configuration - template
#
# Copy this to config.toml and edit for your target. The skill NEVER reads
# literal paths, flags, VLENs, hostnames, or CPU names from SKILL.md or from
# scripts under script/. Everything flows through this file.
#
# Adding a new VPU configuration:
#   1. Drop the Andes Verilog config file under fpga_config/
#   2. If the microarchitecture family is new, add references/uarch/<soc>.md
#   3. Edit [target] to point at the new .inc and uarch doc
#   4. Adjust [target.cflags_base] and [qemu].cpu / [qemu].vlen
#
# No SKILL.md or script edits should ever be needed for a new target.

################################################################################
# [repo] - where libnn source lives and where FSM state is persisted
################################################################################
[repo]
# Absolute path to the libnn source tree (build.sh, Source/, Include/, etc.)
libnn_root = "/home/nick/work/libnn_0421"

# State file location, relative to libnn_root (skill writes FSM state here)
state_file = ".claude/skills/optimize/state.json"

# Work directory for intermediate artifacts (compiled ELFs, objdumps, logs),
# relative to libnn_root
work_dir = ".claude/skills/optimize/work"


################################################################################
# [toolchain] - cross-compiler
################################################################################
[toolchain]
bin_dir = "/local/nick/SW_Release/build-ast540/build-toolchain/linux/nds64le-elf-newlib-v5d/bin"
prefix  = "riscv64-elf-"


################################################################################
# [target] - which VPU, its hardware profile, and its build flags
################################################################################
[target]
# Target CPU name (passed to build.sh)
cpu = "AX45MPV"

# Andes Verilog config file (parsed for VLEN, caches, ISA feature flags).
# Relative to the skill directory (.claude/skills/optimize/).
fpga_config = "fpga_config/ax45mpv_v1024_8mb.inc"

# Microarchitecture reference doc (pipeline model, stall taxonomy).
# Proposer subagent reads this during `analyze`. Relative to skill dir.
uarch_ref = "references/uarch/andes_45_series.md"

# Intrinsic API reference doc (which macros are legal in new RVV code).
# Implement subagent reads this. Relative to skill dir.
intrinsic_api = "references/intrinsic_api/nds_vec_macros.md"


# CFLAGS assembled from three sources (order preserved, concatenated):
#   cflags_base    - baseline compile flags (both verify and perf builds)
#   cflags_perf    - extra flags for FPGA perf build (adds PF_COUNTER, etc.)
#   cflags_verify  - extra flags for QEMU correctness build

[target.cflags_base]
# Flags extracted from build.sh's AX45MPV block
flags = [
  "-O3", "-ffunction-sections", "-fdata-sections", "-Wall", "-Werror",
  "-mtune=andes-45-series", "-fno-strict-aliasing",
  "-mext-vector", "-DENA_VEC_ISA",
  "-DMAX_VLEN=1024", "-DNDS_VEC_RVV_VERSION=1000",
  "-DENA_NDS_V5_VEC_DOT_PROD",
  "-fno-tree-slp-vectorize", "-fno-tree-vectorize",
]

[target.cflags_perf]
flags = [
  "-DPF_COUNTER", "-DENA_TEST_PERF", "-DENA_MEASURE_PERF=1",
  "-DENA_RUN_TWICE", "-DENA_CACHEABLE_ENV_SETUP",
  "-mvh",
]

[target.cflags_verify]
flags = [
  "-DENA_FAST_ALGO", "-DENA_DUMP_GOLDEN=0", "-DENA_MEASURE_PERF=0",
  "-mext-vector=zve64d", "-mvh",
]


[target.active_defines]
# Macros analyze/implement subagents treat as defined or undefined for this
# build (governs which #ifdef branches are live source vs dead code).
defined   = ["ENA_VEC_ISA", "ENA_TILING", "ENA_FAST_ALGO",
             "ENA_NDS_V5_VEC_DOT_PROD"]
undefined = ["ENA_VEC_INTRINSIC", "ENA_VEC_ISA_ZVQMAC"]


################################################################################
# [qemu] - correctness simulator (ground truth: accuracy only, not perf)
################################################################################
[qemu]
binary  = "/local/nick/qemu_v5/build/qemu-system-riscv64"
machine = "andes_ae350"
cpu     = "andes-ax45mpv,vext_spec=v1.0,vlen=1024"
# QEMU device config, relative to libnn_root
config  = "project/ax45mpv/qemu_cfg/zve64d/ADP-AE350-AX45MPV-1C_dw0_mw1.cfg"
vlen    = 1024
timeout = 300


################################################################################
# [fpga] - ground truth for cycle counts
################################################################################
[fpga]
host    = "sw-boards.andestech.com"
port    = 1113
timeout = 300

# Linker script + sim-wrapper + reset flag, all relative to libnn_root
linker_script   = "project/ax45mpv/ae350-xip_hvm.ld"
sim_wrapper     = "project/ax45mpv/riscv64-sim-wrapper-on-board"
reset_flag_file = "project/ax45mpv/First_Reset_done"


################################################################################
# [limits] - FSM exit conditions and cache TTLs
################################################################################
[limits]
# Max optimization rounds per function
max_rounds = 10
# Re-validation cache: skip validate state if config + .inc unchanged and
# last validation was more recent than this (hours). 0 = always re-validate.
validate_cache_hours = 24


################################################################################
# [meta_harness] - Phase 0 trace emission for the outer harness-critic loop
################################################################################
[meta_harness]
# Master toggle for emit_trace.sh. When false, all `init`/`step`/`timing`/`fail`
# calls short-circuit with exit 0 and write nothing to trace_corpus/. FSM
# behavior is unaffected (trace emission is additive by design).
trace_enabled = false

---
name: hot-inner-kernel-extractor
description: Extract a libnn function's hot inner-most loop into a standalone, mcycle-wrapped RISC-V assembly harness so it can be measured on a cycle-accurate Verilator simulator (or any cycle counter) without running the whole function. Use this skill whenever the user wants to study pipeline behavior of a libnn kernel, profile inner-loop cycles, build a microbenchmark from an existing function, prepare a Verilator workload, isolate a hot loop body, or asks about "extracting", "isolating", "harnessing", "microbenchmark", "Verilator timeview", "pipeline timeview", "steady state", or "inner kernel" of any libnn / RVV function. The skill identifies the hottest translation block via QEMU's hotblocks plugin, walks objdump to find the innermost backward branch, computes live-in registers, and emits a compilable .S harness that runs the body N times under csrr mcycle.
allowed-tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash, Edit, Write, Agent
---

# hot-inner-kernel-extractor

Produces a self-contained, cycle-measurable assembly harness from a libnn
function's hot inner loop. Designed for cases where running the full function
on a cycle-accurate simulator (Verilator at ~9 kHz, RTL emulators, etc.) is
too slow to reach steady state -- by extracting just the inner kernel and
wrapping it in a counted measurement loop, you get pipeline-level cycle data
in seconds instead of hours.

## When to use

- User wants to profile pipeline behavior of an existing libnn kernel on
  Verilator and the full function is too slow to simulate.
- User wants a microbenchmark of a specific GEMM/conv/elementwise inner loop
  for cycle-accurate analysis or RTL bring-up.
- User asks "what's the inner kernel of `<fn>`" or "where does `<fn>` spend
  its cycles" -- the skill answers both, with a runnable artifact.

## When NOT to use

- For the FPGA cycle ground truth used by the `/optimize` loop -- that's
  whole-function measurement and is already automated by `/optimize`.
- For correctness debugging -- this skill produces a harness that exercises
  only the hot loop in isolation and does NOT verify outputs against golden.

## Quick start

```bash
# All-in-one: profile, find loop, emit harness + report
LIB_ROOT=$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)
SCRIPT_DIR="${LIB_ROOT}/.claude/skills/hot-inner-kernel-extractor/script"
/usr/bin/bash "${SCRIPT_DIR}/extract_kernel.sh" <function_name>
```

Outputs land under `<skill>/work/<function_name>/`:
- `analysis.json` -- machine-readable: hot block PC, loop boundaries,
  live-ins, prologue, top hotblocks, etc.
- `analysis.md`   -- human-readable report with the loop body, live-in
  list, prologue, and a heuristic-init harness explainer
- `harness.s`     -- standalone Verilator/QEMU harness (bare-metal `_start`,
  internal `csrr mcycle` reads, `cycle_total` symbol for memory scrape)
- `kernel.s` + `kernel.c` -- FPGA-mode harness: `kernel.s` defines a
  callable `extracted_kernel()`; `kernel.c` is a tiny main that uses
  libnn's `nds_pfcounter.h` macros (`_startPFM`/`_stopPFM`/`readResult`)
  to bracket a second call (the first call serves as warmup, matching
  libnn's `ENA_RUN_TWICE` convention). Output format matches every other
  libnn perf test verbatim (`The cycle count is N` / `The inst count is N`),
  so existing grep pipelines parse it unchanged. Run via
  `script/run_kernel_fpga.sh <fn>`.

## Pipeline (FSM-style, but executed straight through)

```
resolve_fn -> ensure_libnn_built -> profile_qemu_hotblocks
    -> analyze_objdump_loop -> emit_harness -> write_report
```

The skill is one-shot: each invocation produces a fresh artifact set. No
state persists across calls (unlike the `/optimize` skill, which has an FSM
across rounds).

### Step 1: resolve function

Given `<function_name>`, resolve to:
- The unit test name (strip `riscv_nn_` prefix if present)
- The unit test source under `Examples/unit_func/t_<name>.c`

Implementation: `extract_kernel.sh` does the lookup. If the test doesn't
exist, the skill exits early with a clear error.

### Step 2: ensure libnn build artifacts

The skill needs:
- `${CFG_LIBNN_ROOT}/libnn.a`
- `${CFG_WORK_DIR}/t_<name>_perf.objdump` (from optimize/run_fpga_test.sh)

If either is missing, the skill calls into `optimize/script/build_libnn.sh`
and `optimize/script/run_fpga_test.sh` to produce them. This means the
skill REUSES the `/optimize` build infrastructure -- there's no parallel
build path to maintain.

### Step 3: profile via QEMU hotblocks

`run_hotblocks.sh <test>` compiles the unit test (or reuses a cached .adx),
runs it under QEMU with `-plugin libhotblocks.so,inline=true`, and dumps
`(pc, tcount, icount, ecount)` per translation block to a plugin log.

The hotblocks plugin counts every basic block QEMU sees, ranked by exec
count. The hottest block is the body of the innermost loop, regardless of
whether that loop is in the user's function or in a callee (e.g. a GEMM
sub-kernel). This matters for libnn because most conv functions dispatch
to `nn_mat_mult_kernel_*` -- the inner kernel is in the callee, not the
top-level conv.

### Step 4: analyze (find_hot_loop.py)

Inputs: hotblocks log + objdump + user's function name.

Algorithm:
1. Parse objdump into (functions, instructions). Each function has a PC
   range; each instruction is keyed by PC.
2. Compute the call graph reachable from the user's function (BFS over
   `jal`/`c.jal` targets that resolve to known symbols). This filters out
   the test harness's I/O loops, which would otherwise dominate ecount.
3. Pick the highest-ecount block whose containing function is in the
   reachable set.
4. From the hot PC, walk forward to find the innermost backward branch
   that closes a loop containing it. The branch's target is the loop
   header; the branch itself is the loop tail.
5. Live-in analysis: for each instruction in the loop body, classify its
   read/write registers. A register is a live-in if it is read before
   being written within the loop. RMW vector instructions (vd4dots,
   vmacc, vredsum, etc.) count their first operand as both read and
   written -- so accumulators show up as live-ins (they expect an initial
   value).
6. Walk the prologue (function entry .. loop header) backwards, keeping
   only instructions that produce live-in values (or values transitively
   consumed by such producers). This is the "producer subset" -- a
   pruned prologue.

Andes vendor extensions (`nds.vd4dots.vv`, `nds.lea.h`, etc.) are handled
by stripping the `nds.` prefix before classification.

### Step 5: emit harness (emit_harness.py)

Produces a `.S` file with:
- `.data` scratch buffers: `scratch_in`, `scratch_wt`, `scratch_out`
- `_start:`
  - small stack init
  - **live-in initialization** (heuristic): scalar pointer-like names
    point at `scratch_in`, scalar count-like names get `0x100`, vector
    accumulators (v0..v15) zeroed, vector activations (v16..v31) loaded
    from `scratch_in`
  - **warmup loop** (default 5 iters, unmeasured)
  - **measurement loop** (default 100 iters) wrapped in
    `csrr mcycle ... csrr mcycle`
  - cycle delta stored to a known symbol `cycle_total` for testbench
    scraping
  - `_exit:` spin label

Each measured iteration RESETS scalar live-ins (so pointers don't walk
off the buffer). Vector live-ins are NOT reset -- this is deliberate:
software-pipelined kernels that consume v_lo/produce v_hi (or vice
versa) need the body's writes to restore the inputs the next iter
expects.

The captured loop body is emitted **verbatim** from objdump, except the
backward branch at `loop_end_pc` is stripped (replaced by our outer
counter's `bnez`).

### Step 6: write report (write_report.py)

Renders `analysis.md` -- the user-facing artifact. Explains what was
found, why each piece was chosen, and how to assemble + run the harness.
Includes the loop body, live-in list, top hotblocks table, and prologue
fold-outs.

## Heuristic init -- read this before trusting the harness

The harness's live-in initialization is a heuristic. It works for the
common GEMM/conv inner loop pattern but can be wrong if:

- A scalar reg points at the *output* buffer (heuristic assumes input)
- A scalar reg is a *count* whose value drives a forward early-exit
  branch in the loop body (e.g. `blt rd, rs1, exit` -- if rd starts low
  the branch fires and the loop exits before we measure)
- A vector reg holds a *broadcast constant* (e.g. NN_ROUND, in_offset)
  rather than tile data

Always inspect the comment-flagged `# heuristic` lines in the generated
harness. The `analysis.md` report includes the verbatim prologue from
the function entry, which you can splice into the harness if heuristic
init produces wrong cycle counts.

## What the skill does NOT do

- Cycle-accurate simulation: that's Verilator's job. The skill produces
  the workload; you run it.
- Memory model fidelity: scratch buffers are flat arrays in `.data`. If
  your TB models DRAM/L2 latency, the cycle count includes those. If
  your TB uses TCM/single-cycle BRAM, you're measuring pipeline only --
  which is the intended use case for pipeline tuning.
- Correctness verification: the harness does not check outputs. Use
  `/optimize`'s verify step or a manual QEMU run for that.

## Files

```
hot-inner-kernel-extractor/
├── SKILL.md             -- this file
├── config.toml          -- symlink to ../optimize/config.toml
├── script/
│   ├── parse_config.sh  -- thin wrapper around optimize/parse_config.sh
│   ├── run_hotblocks.sh -- QEMU + hotblocks plugin runner
│   ├── find_hot_loop.py -- core analysis: hot block, loop, live-ins
│   ├── emit_harness.py  -- .S file emitter
│   ├── write_report.py  -- analysis.md renderer
│   └── extract_kernel.sh -- orchestrator
└── work/                -- per-function output dirs (gitignored)
```

## Rules

1. **Reuse `/optimize`'s build pipeline.** Never duplicate `build_libnn.sh`
   or `run_fpga_test.sh` -- delegate to them.
2. **Filter hotblocks by call graph.** Without filtering, the test harness's
   fread/memcmp loops dominate ecount and the wrong block is picked.
3. **Strip ONLY the backward branch from the loop body.** Mid-loop forward
   branches are kept but rewritten: targets inside the body get local
   labels `_kpc_<hex>:`; targets that escape get redirected to `_oob_exit`,
   a tripwire that poisons `cycle_total` to `-1` so the testbench can
   detect "init was wrong" instead of executing garbage.
4. **Reset scalar live-ins each measured iter, not vector live-ins.** The
   reason: SW-pipeline kernels need vector regs carried iter-to-iter.
5. **Heuristic init is a starting point.** Always remind the user (in the
   report) which lines need spot-checking.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
################################################################################
# Compile the FPGA-mode harness (kernel.s + wrapper.c), upload it to the
# Andes board via the GDB sim wrapper, and parse cycle/inst counts from the
# nds_pfcounter readResult() output -- the same format every libnn perf test
# emits, so the existing grep pipeline works unchanged.
#
# Usage:
#   run_kernel_fpga.sh <function_name>
#
# Inputs (auto-discovered under work/<fn>/):
#   kernel.s    -- emitted by emit_harness.py --mode fpga
#   wrapper.c   -- emitted by emit_harness.py --mode fpga
#
# Output:
#   work/<fn>/kernel.adx       -- linked ELF
#   work/<fn>/kernel.objdump   -- disassembly
#   work/<fn>/fpga_kernel.log  -- raw GDB log
#   parsed cycle/inst counts printed to stdout
################################################################################
set -euo pipefail

SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")" && pwd)"

FN=""
TIMEOUT_OVERRIDE=""

while [[ $# -gt 0 ]]; do
    case "$1" in
        --timeout) TIMEOUT_OVERRIDE="$2"; shift 2 ;;
        -*)        echo "Unknown option: $1" >&2; exit 1 ;;
        *)         FN="$1"; shift ;;
    esac
done

[ -n "${FN}" ] || { echo "Usage: $0 <function_name>" >&2; exit 1; }

# shellcheck disable=SC1091
source "${SCRIPT_DIR}/parse_config.sh"

OUT_DIR="${CFG_HIK_WORK_DIR}/${FN}"
KERNEL_S="${OUT_DIR}/kernel.s"
# emit_harness.py defaults the wrapper to <kernel.s stem>.c == kernel.c
WRAPPER_C="${OUT_DIR}/kernel.c"
ELF="${OUT_DIR}/kernel.adx"
OBJDUMP_FILE="${OUT_DIR}/kernel.objdump"
LOG="${OUT_DIR}/fpga_kernel.log"
TIMEOUT="${TIMEOUT_OVERRIDE:-${CFG_FPGA_TIMEOUT}}"

for f in "${KERNEL_S}" "${WRAPPER_C}"; do
    [ -f "$f" ] || {
        echo "ERROR: Not found: $f" >&2
        echo "       Run extract_kernel.sh --fpga ${FN} first." >&2
        exit 1
    }
done
for f in "${CFG_CC}" "${CFG_FPGA_SIM_WRAPPER}" "${CFG_FPGA_LD_SCRIPT}"; do
    [ -f "$f" ] || { echo "ERROR: Not found: $f" >&2; exit 1; }
done

# GDB-nopython alias trick (same as run_fpga_test.sh).
GDB_NOPY="${CFG_TOOLCHAIN_BIN}/${CFG_TOOLCHAIN_PREFIX}gdb-nopython"
GDB_EXTRA_PATH=""
if [ -f "${CFG_GDB}" ] && [ ! -e "${GDB_NOPY}" ]; then
    GDB_TMPDIR=$(mktemp -d /tmp/hik_fpga_gdb_XXXXXX)
    ln -sf "${CFG_GDB}" "${GDB_TMPDIR}/${CFG_TOOLCHAIN_PREFIX}gdb-nopython"
    GDB_EXTRA_PATH="${GDB_TMPDIR}:"
fi

export PATH="${GDB_EXTRA_PATH}${CFG_TOOLCHAIN_BIN}:${CFG_LIBNN_ROOT}:${PATH}"
export MYPC="${CFG_FPGA_HOST}"
export MYPORT="${CFG_FPGA_PORT}"

# Same INC + flags as run_fpga_test.sh -- needed so nds_pfcounter.h, intrinsics,
# and the linker script all resolve consistently with libnn's other perf tests.
UNIT_TEST_INC="${CFG_LIBNN_ROOT}/Examples/unit_func/include"
INC="-I${CFG_LIBNN_ROOT}/Include -I${UNIT_TEST_INC} -I${CFG_TOOLCHAIN_BIN}/../riscv64-elf/include"
CFLAGS_LINK="-Wl,-gc-sections"
CFLAGS_ALL="${CFG_CFLAGS_BASE} ${CFG_CFLAGS_PERF} ${CFLAGS_LINK}"

echo "[compile] ${FN} (FPGA mode: kernel.s + wrapper.c)"
${CFG_CC} ${CFLAGS_ALL} ${INC} -o "${ELF}" "${WRAPPER_C}" "${KERNEL_S}" -lm 2>&1
${CFG_OBJDUMP} -d "${ELF}" > "${OBJDUMP_FILE}" 2>/dev/null || true

# Reset board state (same as run_fpga_test.sh).
[ -z "${CFG_FPGA_RESET_FLAG}" ] || rm -f "${CFG_FPGA_RESET_FLAG}"

echo "[run] FPGA board ${MYPC}:${MYPORT} (timeout=${TIMEOUT}s)"
timeout "${TIMEOUT}" "${CFG_FPGA_SIM_WRAPPER}" "${ELF}" 2>&1 | tee "${LOG}"
FPGA_RC=${PIPESTATUS[0]}

echo
if [ "${FPGA_RC}" -eq 124 ]; then
    echo "RESULT: TIMEOUT (${TIMEOUT}s)"
    exit 1
fi

CYCLE_COUNT=$(grep -o "The cycle count is [0-9]*" "${LOG}" | tail -1 | grep -o "[0-9]*" || echo "")
INST_COUNT=$( grep -o "The inst count is [0-9]*"  "${LOG}" | tail -1 | grep -o "[0-9]*" || echo "")
ITERS_HINT=$( grep -o "Inner-loop iterations per call: [0-9]*" "${LOG}" | tail -1 | grep -o "[0-9]*$" || echo "")

if [ -n "${CYCLE_COUNT}" ]; then
    echo "--- FPGA Kernel Results ---"
    echo "  Cycles (one measured call): ${CYCLE_COUNT}"
    echo "  Instructions:               ${INST_COUNT:-?}"
    if [ -n "${INST_COUNT}" ] && [ "${CYCLE_COUNT}" -gt 0 ]; then
        IPC=$(python3 -c "print(f'{${INST_COUNT}/${CYCLE_COUNT}:.2f}')" 2>/dev/null || echo "?")
        echo "  IPC:                        ${IPC}"
    fi
    if [ -n "${ITERS_HINT}" ] && [ "${ITERS_HINT}" -gt 0 ]; then
        CPI=$(python3 -c "print(f'{${CYCLE_COUNT}/${ITERS_HINT}:.2f}')")
        echo "  Cycles per inner-loop iter: ${CPI}  (over ${ITERS_HINT} iters)"
    fi
    echo "  ELF:                        ${ELF}"
    echo "  Objdump:                    ${OBJDUMP_FILE}"
    echo "---"
    echo "RESULT: PASS (cycles=${CYCLE_COUNT})"
    exit 0
else
    echo "RESULT: UNKNOWN (no cycle count parsed)"
    echo "--- log tail ---"
    tail -30 "${LOG}" 2>/dev/null || true
    exit 1
fi

skills.name: skill-creator
skills.content:
Base directory for this skill: /home/nick/.claude/skills/skill-creator

# Skill Creator

A skill for creating new skills and iteratively improving them.

At a high level, the process of creating a skill goes like this:

- Decide what you want the skill to do and roughly how it should do it
- Write a draft of the skill
- Create a few test prompts and run claude-with-access-to-the-skill on them
- Help the user evaluate the results both qualitatively and quantitatively
  - While the runs happen in the background, draft some quantitative evals if there aren't any (if there are some, you can either use as is or modify if you feel something needs to change about them). Then explain them to the user (or if they already existed, explain the ones that already exist)
  - Use the `eval-viewer/generate_review.py` script to show the user the results for them to look at, and also let them look at the quantitative metrics
- Rewrite the skill based on feedback from the user's evaluation of the results (and also if there are any glaring flaws that become apparent from the quantitative benchmarks)
- Repeat until you're satisfied
- Expand the test set and try again at larger scale

Your job when using this skill is to figure out where the user is in this process and then jump in and help them progress through these stages. So for instance, maybe they're like "I want to make a skill for X". You can help narrow down what they mean, write a draft, write the test cases, figure out how they want to evaluate, run all the prompts, and repeat.

On the other hand, maybe they already have a draft of the skill. In this case you can go straight to the eval/iterate part of the loop.

Of course, you should always be flexible and if the user is like "I don't need to run a bunch of evaluations, just vibe with me", you can do that instead.

Then after the skill is done (but again, the order is flexible), you can also run the skill description improver, which we have a whole separate script for, to optimize the triggering of the skill.

Cool? Cool.

## Communicating with the user

The skill creator is liable to be used by people across a wide range of familiarity with coding jargon. If you haven't heard (and how could you, it's only very recently that it started), there's a trend now where the power of Claude is inspiring plumbers to open up their terminals, parents and grandparents to google "how to install npm". On the other hand, the bulk of users are probably fairly computer-literate.

So please pay attention to context cues to understand how to phrase your communication! In the default case, just to give you some idea:

- "evaluation" and "benchmark" are borderline, but OK
- for "JSON" and "assertion" you want to see serious cues from the user that they know what those things are before using them without explaining them

It's OK to briefly explain terms if you're in doubt, and feel free to clarify terms with a short definition if you're unsure if the user will get it.

---

## Creating a skill

### Capture Intent

Start by understanding the user's intent. The current conversation might already contain a workflow the user wants to capture (e.g., they say "turn this into a skill"). If so, extract answers from the conversation history first — the tools used, the sequence of steps, corrections the user made, input/output formats observed. The user may need to fill the gaps, and should confirm before proceeding to the next step.

1. What should this skill enable Claude to do?
2. When should this skill trigger? (what user phrases/contexts)
3. What's the expected output format?
4. Should we set up test cases to verify the skill works? Skills with objectively verifiable outputs (file transforms, data extraction, code generation, fixed workflow steps) benefit from test cases. Skills with subjective outputs (writing style, art) often don't need them. Suggest the appropriate default based on the skill type, but let the user decide.

### Interview and Research

Proactively ask questions about edge cases, input/output formats, example files, success criteria, and dependencies. Wait to write test prompts until you've got this part ironed out.

Check available MCPs - if useful for research (searching docs, finding similar skills, looking up best practices), research in parallel via subagents if available, otherwise inline. Come prepared with context to reduce burden on the user.

### Write the SKILL.md

Based on the user interview, fill in these components:

- **name**: Skill identifier
- **description**: When to trigger, what it does. This is the primary triggering mechanism - include both what the skill does AND specific contexts for when to use it. All "when to use" info goes here, not in the body. Note: currently Claude has a tendency to "undertrigger" skills -- to not use them when they'd be useful. To combat this, please make the skill descriptions a little bit "pushy". So for instance, instead of "How to build a simple fast dashboard to display internal Anthropic data.", you might write "How to build a simple fast dashboard to display internal Anthropic data. Make sure to use this skill whenever the user mentions dashboards, data visualization, internal metrics, or wants to display any kind of company data, even if they don't explicitly ask for a 'dashboard.'"
- **compatibility**: Required tools, dependencies (optional, rarely needed)
- **the rest of the skill :)**

### Skill Writing Guide

#### Anatomy of a Skill

```
skill-name/
├── SKILL.md (required)
│   ├── YAML frontmatter (name, description required)
│   └── Markdown instructions
└── Bundled Resources (optional)
    ├── scripts/    - Executable code for deterministic/repetitive tasks
    ├── references/ - Docs loaded into context as needed
    └── assets/     - Files used in output (templates, icons, fonts)
```

#### Progressive Disclosure

Skills use a three-level loading system:
1. **Metadata** (name + description) - Always in context (~100 words)
2. **SKILL.md body** - In context whenever skill triggers (<500 lines ideal)
3. **Bundled resources** - As needed (unlimited, scripts can execute without loading)

These word counts are approximate and you can feel free to go longer if needed.

**Key patterns:**
- Keep SKILL.md under 500 lines; if you're approaching this limit, add an additional layer of hierarchy along with clear pointers about where the model using the skill should go next to follow up.
- Reference files clearly from SKILL.md with guidance on when to read them
- For large reference files (>300 lines), include a table of contents

**Domain organization**: When a skill supports multiple domains/frameworks, organize by variant:
```
cloud-deploy/
├── SKILL.md (workflow + selection)
└── references/
    ├── aws.md
    ├── gcp.md
    └── azure.md
```
Claude reads only the relevant reference file.

#### Principle of Lack of Surprise

This goes without saying, but skills must not contain malware, exploit code, or any content that could compromise system security. A skill's contents should not surprise the user in their intent if described. Don't go along with requests to create misleading skills or skills designed to facilitate unauthorized access, data exfiltration, or other malicious activities. Things like a "roleplay as an XYZ" are OK though.

#### Writing Patterns

Prefer using the imperative form in instructions.

**Defining output formats** - You can do it like this:
```markdown
## Report structure
ALWAYS use this exact template:
# [Title]
## Executive summary
## Key findings
## Recommendations
```

**Examples pattern** - It's useful to include examples. You can format them like this (but if "Input" and "Output" are in the examples you might want to deviate a little):
```markdown
## Commit message format
**Example 1:**
Input: Added user authentication with JWT tokens
Output: feat(auth): implement JWT-based authentication
```

### Writing Style

Try to explain to the model why things are important in lieu of heavy-handed musty MUSTs. Use theory of mind and try to make the skill general and not super-narrow to specific examples. Start by writing a draft and then look at it with fresh eyes and improve it.

### Test Cases

After writing the skill draft, come up with 2-3 realistic test prompts — the kind of thing a real user would actually say. Share them with the user: [you don't have to use this exact language] "Here are a few test cases I'd like to try. Do these look right, or do you want to add more?" Then run them.

Save test cases to `evals/evals.json`. Don't write assertions yet — just the prompts. You'll draft assertions in the next step while the runs are in progress.

```json
{
  "skill_name": "example-skill",
  "evals": [
    {
      "id": 1,
      "prompt": "User's task prompt",
      "expected_output": "Description of expected result",
      "files": []
    }
  ]
}
```

See `references/schemas.md` for the full schema (including the `assertions` field, which you'll add later).

## Running and evaluating test cases

This section is one continuous sequence — don't stop partway through. Do NOT use `/skill-test` or any other testing skill.

Put results in `<skill-name>-workspace/` as a sibling to the skill directory. Within the workspace, organize results by iteration (`iteration-1/`, `iteration-2/`, etc.) and within that, each test case gets a directory (`eval-0/`, `eval-1/`, etc.). Don't create all of this upfront — just create directories as you go.

### Step 1: Spawn all runs (with-skill AND baseline) in the same turn

For each test case, spawn two subagents in the same turn — one with the skill, one without. This is important: don't spawn the with-skill runs first and then come back for baselines later. Launch everything at once so it all finishes around the same time.

**With-skill run:**

```
Execute this task:
- Skill path: <path-to-skill>
- Task: <eval prompt>
- Input files: <eval files if any, or "none">
- Save outputs to: <workspace>/iteration-<N>/eval-<ID>/with_skill/outputs/
- Outputs to save: <what the user cares about — e.g., "the .docx file", "the final CSV">
```

**Baseline run** (same prompt, but the baseline depends on context):
- **Creating a new skill**: no skill at all. Same prompt, no skill path, save to `without_skill/outputs/`.
- **Improving an existing skill**: the old version. Before editing, snapshot the skill (`cp -r <skill-path> <workspace>/skill-snapshot/`), then point the baseline subagent at the snapshot. Save to `old_skill/outputs/`.

Write an `eval_metadata.json` for each test case (assertions can be empty for now). Give each eval a descriptive name based on what it's testing — not just "eval-0". Use this name for the directory too. If this iteration uses new or modified eval prompts, create these files for each new eval directory — don't assume they carry over from previous iterations.

```json
{
  "eval_id": 0,
  "eval_name": "descriptive-name-here",
  "prompt": "The user's task prompt",
  "assertions": []
}
```

### Step 2: While runs are in progress, draft assertions

Don't just wait for the runs to finish — you can use this time productively. Draft quantitative assertions for each test case and explain them to the user. If assertions already exist in `evals/evals.json`, review them and explain what they check.

Good assertions are objectively verifiable and have descriptive names — they should read clearly in the benchmark viewer so someone glancing at the results immediately understands what each one checks. Subjective skills (writing style, design quality) are better evaluated qualitatively — don't force assertions onto things that need human judgment.

Update the `eval_metadata.json` files and `evals/evals.json` with the assertions once drafted. Also explain to the user what they'll see in the viewer — both the qualitative outputs and the quantitative benchmark.

### Step 3: As runs complete, capture timing data

When each subagent task completes, you receive a notification containing `total_tokens` and `duration_ms`. Save this data immediately to `timing.json` in the run directory:

```json
{
  "total_tokens": 84852,
  "duration_ms": 23332,
  "total_duration_seconds": 23.3
}
```

This is the only opportunity to capture this data — it comes through the task notification and isn't persisted elsewhere. Process each notification as it arrives rather than trying to batch them.

### Step 4: Grade, aggregate, and launch the viewer

Once all runs are done:

1. **Grade each run** — spawn a grader subagent (or grade inline) that reads `agents/grader.md` and evaluates each assertion against the outputs. Save results to `grading.json` in each run directory. The grading.json expectations array must use the fields `text`, `passed`, and `evidence` (not `name`/`met`/`details` or other variants) — the viewer depends on these exact field names. For assertions that can be checked programmatically, write and run a script rather than eyeballing it — scripts are faster, more reliable, and can be reused across iterations.

2. **Aggregate into benchmark** — run the aggregation script from the skill-creator directory:
   ```bash
   python -m scripts.aggregate_benchmark <workspace>/iteration-N --skill-name <name>
   ```
   This produces `benchmark.json` and `benchmark.md` with pass_rate, time, and tokens for each configuration, with mean ± stddev and the delta. If generating benchmark.json manually, see `references/schemas.md` for the exact schema the viewer expects.
Put each with_skill version before its baseline counterpart.

3. **Do an analyst pass** — read the benchmark data and surface patterns the aggregate stats might hide. See `agents/analyzer.md` (the "Analyzing Benchmark Results" section) for what to look for — things like assertions that always pass regardless of skill (non-discriminating), high-variance evals (possibly flaky), and time/token tradeoffs.

4. **Launch the viewer** with both qualitative outputs and quantitative data:
   ```bash
   nohup python <skill-creator-path>/eval-viewer/generate_review.py \
     <workspace>/iteration-N \
     --skill-name "my-skill" \
     --benchmark <workspace>/iteration-N/benchmark.json \
     > /dev/null 2>&1 &
   VIEWER_PID=$!
   ```
   For iteration 2+, also pass `--previous-workspace <workspace>/iteration-<N-1>`.

   **Cowork / headless environments:** If `webbrowser.open()` is not available or the environment has no display, use `--static <output_path>` to write a standalone HTML file instead of starting a server. Feedback will be downloaded as a `feedback.json` file when the user clicks "Submit All Reviews". After download, copy `feedback.json` into the workspace directory for the next iteration to pick up.

Note: please use generate_review.py to create the viewer; there's no need to write custom HTML.

5. **Tell the user** something like: "I've opened the results in your browser. There are two tabs — 'Outputs' lets you click through each test case and leave feedback, 'Benchmark' shows the quantitative comparison. When you're done, come back here and let me know."

### What the user sees in the viewer

The "Outputs" tab shows one test case at a time:
- **Prompt**: the task that was given
- **Output**: the files the skill produced, rendered inline where possible
- **Previous Output** (iteration 2+): collapsed section showing last iteration's output
- **Formal Grades** (if grading was run): collapsed section showing assertion pass/fail
- **Feedback**: a textbox that auto-saves as they type
- **Previous Feedback** (iteration 2+): their comments from last time, shown below the textbox

The "Benchmark" tab shows the stats summary: pass rates, timing, and token usage for each configuration, with per-eval breakdowns and analyst observations.

Navigation is via prev/next buttons or arrow keys. When done, they click "Submit All Reviews" which saves all feedback to `feedback.json`.

### Step 5: Read the feedback

When the user tells you they're done, read `feedback.json`:

```json
{
  "reviews": [
    {"run_id": "eval-0-with_skill", "feedback": "the chart is missing axis labels", "timestamp": "..."},
    {"run_id": "eval-1-with_skill", "feedback": "", "timestamp": "..."},
    {"run_id": "eval-2-with_skill", "feedback": "perfect, love this", "timestamp": "..."}
  ],
  "status": "complete"
}
```

Empty feedback means the user thought it was fine. Focus your improvements on the test cases where the user had specific complaints.

Kill the viewer server when you're done with it:

```bash
kill $VIEWER_PID 2>/dev/null
```

---

## Improving the skill

This is the heart of the loop. You've run the test cases, the user has reviewed the results, and now you need to make the skill better based on their feedback.

### How to think about improvements

1. **Generalize from the feedback.** The big picture thing that's happening here is that we're trying to create skills that can be used a million times (maybe literally, maybe even more who knows) across many different prompts. Here you and the user are iterating on only a few examples over and over again because it helps move faster. The user knows these examples in and out and it's quick for them to assess new outputs. But if the skill you and the user are codeveloping works only for those examples, it's useless. Rather than put in fiddly overfitty changes, or oppressively constrictive MUSTs, if there's some stubborn issue, you might try branching out and using different metaphors, or recommending different patterns of working. It's relatively cheap to try and maybe you'll land on something great.

2. **Keep the prompt lean.** Remove things that aren't pulling their weight. Make sure to read the transcripts, not just the final outputs — if it looks like the skill is making the model waste a bunch of time doing things that are unproductive, you can try getting rid of the parts of the skill that are making it do that and seeing what happens.

3. **Explain the why.** Try hard to explain the **why** behind everything you're asking the model to do. Today's LLMs are *smart*. They have good theory of mind and when given a good harness can go beyond rote instructions and really make things happen. Even if the feedback from the user is terse or frustrated, try to actually understand the task and why the user is writing what they wrote, and what they actually wrote, and then transmit this understanding into the instructions. If you find yourself writing ALWAYS or NEVER in all caps, or using super rigid structures, that's a yellow flag — if possible, reframe and explain the reasoning so that the model understands why the thing you're asking for is important. That's a more humane, powerful, and effective approach.

4. **Look for repeated work across test cases.** Read the transcripts from the test runs and notice if the subagents all independently wrote similar helper scripts or took the same multi-step approach to something. If all 3 test cases resulted in the subagent writing a `create_docx.py` or a `build_chart.py`, that's a strong signal the skill should bundle that script. Write it once, put it in `scripts/`, and tell the skill to use it. This saves every future invocation from reinventing the wheel.

This task is pretty important (we are trying to create billions a year in economic value here!) and your thinking time is not the blocker; take your time and really mull things over. I'd suggest writing a draft revision and then looking at it anew and making improvements. Really do your best to get into the head of the user and understand what they want and need.

### The iteration loop

After improving the skill:

1. Apply your improvements to the skill
2. Rerun all test cases into a new `iteration-<N+1>/` directory, including baseline runs. If you're creating a new skill, the baseline is always `without_skill` (no skill) — that stays the same across iterations. If you're improving an exist

| N | Cycles | Inst | cyc/iter | inst/iter | Overhead % |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 10   | 1,797   | 979    | 179.70 | 97.90 | 5.2% |
| 100  | 17,094  | 9,259  | 170.94 | 92.59 | 0.55% |
| 1000 | 170,093 | 92,059 | 170.09 | 92.06 | 0.055% |

**Steady-state** (from delta N=1000 vs N=100):
- 169.999 cyc/iter, exactly 92.0 inst/iter, IPC = 0.541

**Per-call overhead** (170093 - 1000*170.0):
- ~94 cycles, ~59 inst — matches the prior estimate

The numbers converge cleanly: each 10x increase in N halves-then-decimates the apparent cyc/iter toward 170.0. At N=1000 you're within 0.05% of true steady state — good enough that further increases won't move the needle.

message.content: why do we need the 'vector live-ins' proloue? do they affect the loop iteration? we don't care teh value

content.text:
You're right - on AX45MPV the vector pipeline is data-independent, so vector live-in **values** don't affect cycle counts. vd4dots/vmul/vsra/vadd/vmacc all run in fixed cycles regardless of operand bits.

What actually matters before entering the loop:

1. **`vsetvli` state (vl + vtype)** - mandatory. Without it vector insns trap or use wrong VL.
2. **Scalar live-ins** - mandatory:
   - regs driving forward branches (`a3 < s1` early-exit) - wrong value -> loop exits or skips body
   - regs used as load/store addresses - wrong value -> trap or out-of-bounds memory traffic that distorts cycles
3. **Vector live-ins** - **not needed for this kernel**. Reason we emit it anyway:
   - Defensive. Some kernels have mask-driven control flow (`vmsXX` -> mask register -> conditional vector op count) that *can* be data-dependent at the issue level. The harness can't tell from objdump alone whether yours does.
   - For SW-pipelined loops, the body re-writes v16..v31 each iter, so even though we init once, garbage works fine after iter 1.

For your conv kernel specifically the vector init is dead weight - you could replace the per-register `vmv.v.x` / `vle32` block with a single `vsetvli` and the cycle count wouldn't change. That would shrink per-call overhead from ~94 cyc to ~30 cyc.

Want me to add a `--skip-vector-init` flag (or make it the default with `--safe-vector-init` opt-in) so the harness only emits vsetvli + scalar init?

1	#!/usr/bin/env python3
2	"""
3	Emit a self-contained harness for the extracted hot inner loop body.
4	
5	Two modes:
6	
7	  --mode standalone   (default)
8	    Single .s file. Bare-metal _start with internal csrr mcycle reads.
9	    Stores cycles to `cycle_total` and spins at _exit. For Verilator
10	    testbenches that scrape memory by symbol.
11	
12	  --mode fpga
13	    Two files: kernel.s + wrapper.c. The .s defines extracted_kernel() as
14	    a callable function (proper RV ABI prologue/epilogue, scratch buffers
15	    live in .data). The .c defines main() that uses libnn's nds_pfcounter
16	    macros (_startPFM/_stopPFM/readResult) to wrap a second call to
17	    extracted_kernel -- producing exactly the same "The cycle count is N"
18	    output format that Examples/unit_func/* perf tests print, so the
19	    existing run_fpga_test.sh-style cycle-grep pipeline works unchanged.
20	
21	Counter-reg selection:
22	    Both modes need an outer iteration counter (the body's original
23	    backward branch is stripped). The counter must not collide with any
24	    scalar live-in of the body. We prefer callee-saved s* regs (cheap to
25	    save/restore in fpga mode); fall back to caller-saved t2/a6/a7 if
26	    s2..s11 are all busy. This fixes a real bug where `t0` was used as
27	    counter even when t0 was a live-in pointer -- pointer would get
28	    decremented and the loop would exit mid-init.
29	"""
30	
31	import argparse
32	import json
33	import re
34	import sys
35	from pathlib import Path
36	
37	
38	INSN_RE = re.compile(r"^\s*([0-9a-f]+):\s+([0-9a-f]+)\s+(\S+)(?:\s+(.*))?$")
39	
40	
41	def parse_insn_line(line):
42	    m = INSN_RE.match(line)
43	    if not m:
44	        return None
45	    return {
46	        "addr":     int(m.group(1), 16),
47	        "mnemonic": m.group(3),
48	        "operands": (m.group(4) or "").strip(),
49	        "raw":      line,
50	    }
51	
52	
53	# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
54	# Counter register selection
55	# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
56	
57	def pick_counter_reg(live_ins):
58	    """
59	    Pick a scalar register safe to use as the outer iteration counter.
60	
61	    Constraints:
62	      - MUST NOT be in scalar live-ins (the body would clobber it / use it).
63	      - PREFER callee-saved regs (s2..s11). In fpga mode we save/restore
64	        them anyway for ABI compliance, so adding the counter to that list
65	        is free. Caller-saved t/a regs are a fallback.
66	
67	    Returns a register name like "s2".
68	    """
69	    scalar_li = set(live_ins.get("scalar", []))
70	    for cand in ["s2", "s3", "s4", "s5", "s6", "s7", "s8", "s9", "s10", "s11"]:
71	        if cand not in scalar_li:
72	            return cand
73	    for cand in ["t2", "a6", "a7"]:
74	        if cand not in scalar_li:
75	            return cand
76	    raise RuntimeError("no free scalar register left for the outer counter -- "
77	                       "kernel uses every callee/caller-saved scalar")
78	
79	
80	def callee_saved_clobbered(live_ins, counter):
81	    """
82	    Set of callee-saved regs the function will clobber and therefore must
83	    save/restore: live-in scalar s-regs + the counter (if it's an s-reg).
84	
85	    ra and sp aren't included; the prologue/epilogue handles those directly.
86	    """
87	    out = set()
88	    for r in live_ins.get("scalar", []):
89	        if r.startswith("s") and r != "sp":
90	            out.add(r)
91	    if counter.startswith("s"):
92	        out.add(counter)
93	    return sorted(out, key=lambda r: int(r[1:]))
94	
95	
96	# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
97	# Live-in initialization
98	# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
99	
100	SCALAR_PTR_HINT = {
101	    "a1": "out",
102	    "a2": "in",
103	    "a0": "in",
104	}
105	
106	
107	def emit_live_in_init(live_ins):
108	    out = []
109	    out.append("    # ---- live-in initialization ----")
110	    out.append("    # NOTE: scalar pointer assignments are heuristic. Read")
111	    out.append("    # the loop body and adjust labels (scratch_in/_wt/_out)")
112	    out.append("    # if a register actually points at a different buffer.")
113	
114	    for name in live_ins["scalar"]:
115	        if name in ("zero", "x0", "sp", "ra", "tp", "gp"):
116	            continue
117	        # Count/threshold heuristic: many libnn kernels have a "remaining
118	        # count" register (decremented inside the body, e.g. `sub a3,a3,a4`)
119	        # compared against a small threshold (`blt a3, s1, exit`). For the
120	        # mid-loop branch to NOT fire we need decrementing-count >> threshold.
121	        # We bucket by name based on libnn convention:
122	        #   a3            -> remaining count (large positive)
123	        #   a4, a5        -> vl-like outputs from vsetvli (large positive,
124	        #                    will get overwritten by body's own vsetvli)
125	        #   s1, s3, t6    -> threshold-like (zero / small)
126	        # Other count-shaped names get a moderate value as before.
127	        if name in ("a3",):
128	            out.append(f"    li {name}, 0x10000   # remaining count: must stay > threshold")
129	        elif name in ("a4", "a5"):
130	            out.append(f"    li {name}, 0x10000   # vl-like output of vsetvli; large fallback")
131	        elif name in ("s1", "s3", "t6"):
132	            out.append(f"    li {name}, 0   # threshold for mid-loop branch; small")
133	        else:
134	            buf = "scratch_out" if SCALAR_PTR_HINT.get(name) == "out" else \
135	                  "scratch_wt"  if SCALAR_PTR_HINT.get(name) == "wt"  else \
136	                  "scratch_in"
137	            out.append(f"    la {name}, {buf}   # heuristic pointer init")
138	
139	    if live_ins["vector"]:
140	        out.append("    # vector live-ins: zero accumulators, load v16+ from scratch_in")
141	        # Use LMUL=1 here -- with LMUL>1, only group-aligned vd are legal
142	        # (v0,v8,v16,v24 for m8; v0,v4,v8,... for m4) and individual writes
143	        # to v1/v2/etc would trap as illegal-instruction. LMUL=1 lets us
144	        # touch every vector reg one by one. The loop body resets vtype
145	        # via its own vsetvli on entry so this leaves vtype in a
146	        # known-deterministic state for the body to override.
147	        out.append("    vsetvli t0, x0, e8, m1, tu, mu   # one-reg-at-a-time fill")
148	        for vname in live_ins["vector"]:
149	            n = int(vname[1:])
150	            if n < 16:
151	                out.append(f"    vand.vi {vname}, {vname}, 0   # zero accumulator")
152	            else:
153	                out.append(f"    vle8.v {vname}, (a0)   # load from scratch_in (heuristic)")
154	        out.append("    vsetvli x0, x0, e8, m1, tu, mu   # leave vtype in a sane state for body")
155	
156	    return out
157	
158	
159	def emit_live_in_reset(live_ins):
160	    out = []
161	    out.append("    # reset scalar live-ins each iter")
162	    # Reset must mirror init exactly (same values), otherwise the loop body's
163	    # mid-branches see different state on iteration 2 vs iteration 1 and
164	    # may exit early. Keep this in lock-step with emit_live_in_init.
165	    for name in live_ins["scalar"]:
166	        if name in ("zero", "x0", "sp", "ra", "tp", "gp"):
167	            continue
168	        if name in ("a3",):
169	            out.append(f"    li {name}, 0x10000")
170	        elif name in ("a4", "a5"):
171	            out.append(f"    li {name}, 0x10000")
172	        elif name in ("s1", "s3", "t6"):
173	            out.append(f"    li {name}, 0")
174	        else:
175	            buf = "scratch_out" if SCALAR_PTR_HINT.get(name) == "out" else \
176	                  "scratch_wt"  if SCALAR_PTR_HINT.get(name) == "wt"  else \
177	                  "scratch_in"
178	            out.append(f"    la {name}, {buf}")
179	    return out
180	
181	
182	# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
183	# Loop body emission with branch rewriting
184	# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
185	
186	BRANCH_MNEMONICS = {
187	    "beq", "bne", "blt", "bltu", "bge", "bgeu",
188	    "beqz", "bnez", "blez", "bgez", "bltz", "bgtz",
189	    "ble", "bgt", "bleu", "bgtu",
190	    "c.beqz", "c.bnez",
191	    "j", "c.j", "jal", "c.jal",
192	}
193	
194	BRANCH_OPERAND_RE = re.compile(r"^(.*?)(0x[0-9a-f]+)(\s*<[^>]+>)?\s*$")
195	
196	
197	def label_for(addr):
198	    return f"_kpc_{addr:x}"
199	
200	
201	def emit_loop_body(loop_lines, loop_start_pc, loop_end_pc):
202	    body_pcs = set()
203	    targets_inside = set()
204	    for raw in loop_lines:
205	        ins = parse_insn_line(raw)
206	        if ins is None:
207	            continue
208	        body_pcs.add(ins["addr"])
209	
210	    for raw in loop_lines:
211	        ins = parse_insn_line(raw)
212	        if ins is None or ins["mnemonic"] not in BRANCH_MNEMONICS:
213	            continue
214	        if ins["addr"] == loop_end_pc:
215	            continue
216	        m = BRANCH_OPERAND_RE.match(ins["operands"])
217	        if not m:
218	            continue
219	        tgt = int(m.group(2), 16)
220	        if loop_start_pc <= tgt <= loop_end_pc:
221	            targets_inside.add(tgt)
222	
223	    out = []
224	    for raw in loop_lines:
225	        ins = parse_insn_line(raw)
226	        if ins is None:
227	            continue
228	        if ins["addr"] in targets_inside:
229	            out.append(f"{label_for(ins['addr'])}:")
230	
231	        if ins["addr"] == loop_end_pc:
232	            out.append(
233	                f"    # (stripped backward branch: "
234	                f"{ins['mnemonic']} {ins['operands']})"
235	            )
236	            continue
237	
238	        if ins["mnemonic"] in BRANCH_MNEMONICS:
239	            m = BRANCH_OPERAND_RE.match(ins["operands"])
240	            if m:
241	                pre = m.group(1)
242	                tgt = int(m.group(2), 16)
243	                new_tgt = (label_for(tgt)
244	                           if loop_start_pc <= tgt <= loop_end_pc
245	                           else "_oob_exit")
246	                out.append(f"    {ins['mnemonic']} {pre}{new_tgt}")
247	                continue
248	
249	        out.append(f"    {ins['mnemonic']} {ins['operands']}".rstrip())
250	
251	    return out
252	
253	
254	# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
255	# Standalone-mode emission
256	# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
257	
258	STANDALONE_HEADER = """\
259	###############################################################################
260	# Hot-inner-kernel harness (standalone mode)
261	#
262	# Function : {fn}
263	# Hot block: {hot_pc}  (ecount={ecount})
264	# Loop     : {loop_start} .. {loop_end}  ({body_count} insns)
265	# Live-ins : {n_scalar} scalar, {n_vector} vector
266	# Counter  : {counter}  (chosen to avoid live-in collision)
267	###############################################################################
268	
269	    .section .data
270	    .align 6
271	scratch_in:
272	    .skip {bufsize}
273	    .align 6
274	scratch_wt:
275	    .skip {bufsize}
276	    .align 6
277	scratch_out:
278	    .skip {bufsize}
279	
280	    .align 3
281	cycle_total:    .dword 0
282	cycle_warmup:   .dword 0
283	
284	    .section .text
285	    .globl _start
286	_start:
287	    la sp, _stack_top
288	"""
289	
290	STANDALONE_WARMUP = """\
291	
292	    # ---- warmup: settle pipeline state, do NOT measure ----
293	    li {counter}, {warmup}
294	warmup_loop:
295	{reset}
296	{body}
297	    addi {counter}, {counter}, -1
298	    bnez {counter}, warmup_loop
299	"""
300	
301	STANDALONE_MEASURE = """\
302	
303	    # ---- measurement window ----
304	    csrr s10, mcycle
305	    li {counter}, {iters}
306	measure_loop:
307	{reset}
308	{body}
309	    addi {counter}, {counter}, -1
310	    bnez {counter}, measure_loop
311	    csrr s11, mcycle
312	    sub  s11, s11, s10
313	    la   t1, cycle_total
314	    sd   s11, 0(t1)
315	"""
316	
317	STANDALONE_EXIT = """\
318	
319	_exit:
320	1:  j 1b
321	
322	_oob_exit:
323	    la t0, cycle_total
324	    li t1, -1
325	    sd t1, 0(t0)
326	    j _exit
327	
328	    .section .bss
329	    .align 4
330	    .skip 4096
331	_stack_top:
332	"""
333	
334	
335	def emit_standalone(analysis, warmup, iters, bufsize):
336	    live_ins = analysis["live_in_regs"]
337	    counter = pick_counter_reg(live_ins)
338	    loop_start_pc = int(analysis["loop_start_pc"], 16)
339	    loop_end_pc = int(analysis["loop_end_pc"], 16)
340	
341	    init_lines = emit_live_in_init(live_ins)
342	    reset_lines = emit_live_in_reset(live_ins)
343	    body_lines = emit_loop_body(analysis["loop_body_lines"],
344	                                loop_start_pc, loop_end_pc)
345	    indent = lambda lines: "\n".join(lines)
346	
347	    header = STANDALONE_HEADER.format(
348	        fn=analysis["containing_fn"],
349	        hot_pc=analysis["hot_block_pc"],
350	        ecount=analysis["hot_block_ecount"],
351	        loop_start=analysis["loop_start_pc"],
352	        loop_end=analysis["loop_end_pc"],
353	        body_count=analysis["loop_body_count"],
354	        n_scalar=len(live_ins["scalar"]),
355	        n_vector=len(live_ins["vector"]),
356	        bufsize=bufsize,
357	        counter=counter,
358	    )
359	    return "\n".join([
360	        header,
361	        indent(init_lines),
362	        STANDALONE_WARMUP.format(counter=counter, warmup=warmup,
363	                                 reset=indent(reset_lines),
364	                                 body=indent(body_lines)),
365	        STANDALONE_MEASURE.format(counter=counter, iters=iters,
366	                                  reset=indent(reset_lines),
367	                                  body=indent(body_lines)),
368	        STANDALONE_EXIT,
369	    ])
370	
371	
372	# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
373	# FPGA-mode emission
374	# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
375	
376	# In FPGA mode the harness is a regular function callable from C. The wrapper
377	# C source uses libnn's nds_pfcounter.h macros (_startPFM/_stopPFM/readResult)
378	# so output looks identical to existing libnn perf tests, and run_fpga_test.sh
379	# can grep cycle/inst counts from the same lines.
380	
381	FPGA_KERNEL_HEADER = """\
382	###############################################################################
383	# Extracted kernel (FPGA mode)
384	#
385	# Signature: void extracted_kernel(long iters)   /* iters in a0 */
386	#
387	# Function : {fn}
388	# Hot block: {hot_pc}  (ecount={ecount})
389	# Loop     : {loop_start} .. {loop_end}  ({body_count} insns)
390	# Live-ins : {n_scalar} scalar, {n_vector} vector
391	# Counter  : {counter}  (clobbered; saved/restored per RV ABI)
392	#
393	# Iteration count : {iters}  ({body_count}-insn body * {iters} = ~{est_insns} insns/call)
394	#
395	# Pattern (matches libnn's ENA_RUN_TWICE convention):
396	#   call extracted_kernel  -- warmup; PFM not yet started
397	#   _startPFM
398	#   call extracted_kernel  -- measured
399	#   _stopPFM
400	#   readResult             -- prints "The cycle count is N"
401	###############################################################################
402	
403	    .section .data
404	    .align 6
405	scratch_in:
406	    .skip {bufsize}
407	    .align 6
408	scratch_wt:
409	    .skip {bufsize}
410	    .align 6
411	scratch_out:
412	    .skip {bufsize}
413	
414	    .section .text
415	    .globl extracted_kernel
416	    .type  extracted_kernel, @function
417	extracted_kernel:
418	    # ---- prologue: save callee-saved regs we will clobber ----
419	    addi sp, sp, -{frame_size}
420	    sd ra, 0(sp)
421	{save_block}
422	"""
423	
424	FPGA_KERNEL_BODY = """\
425	    # Capture iter count from a0 BEFORE live-in init (which may clobber a0
426	    # via `la a0, scratch_in`). Counter ({counter}) is callee-saved and was
427	    # already stashed in the prologue, so it's safe to use as scratch here.
428	    mv {counter}, a0
429	
430	{init}
431	
432	    # ---- iteration loop ----
433	    beqz {counter}, extracted_kernel_epilogue
434	extracted_kernel_loop:
435	{reset}
436	{body}
437	    addi {counter}, {counter}, -1
438	    bnez {counter}, extracted_kernel_loop
439	
440	    # ---- normal epilogue ----
441	extracted_kernel_epilogue:
442	    ld ra, 0(sp)
443	{restore_block}
444	    addi sp, sp, {frame_size}
445	    ret
446	
447	_oob_exit:
448	    # If init was wrong, a forward branch in the body lands here. Fall
449	    # through to the epilogue so the function still returns -- the caller
450	    # will see a wildly-off cycle count, which is the failure signal.
451	    j extracted_kernel_epilogue
452	
453	    .size extracted_kernel, . - extracted_kernel
454	"""
455	
456	
457	FPGA_WRAPPER_C = '''\
458	/* Auto-generated wrapper for hot-inner-kernel-extractor (FPGA mode).
459	 *
460	 * Uses libnn's nds_pfcounter.h to bracket the measured call with PFM.
461	 * Output format matches every other libnn perf test, so existing grep
462	 * pipelines (like run_fpga_test.sh) parse it without modification.
463	 *
464	 * Function being measured: {fn}
465	 * Warmup iters (unmeasured): {warmup_iters}
466	 * Measured iters:            {iters}
467	 *
468	 * Cycles per inner-loop iter = (printed cycles) / {iters}, modulo a small
469	 * per-call init/epilogue overhead. With warmup_iters >= 5 the measured call
470	 * already starts in pipeline steady state.
471	 */
472	
473	/* CFG_CFLAGS_PERF already adds -DPF_COUNTER; guard to avoid redefinition. */
474	#ifndef PF_COUNTER
475	#define PF_COUNTER
476	#endif
477	#include "nds_pfcounter.h"
478	#include <stdio.h>
479	
480	/* extracted_kernel runs `iters` iterations of the captured loop body, with
481	 * the live-ins re-initialized at the start of each iteration. The C wrapper
482	 * calls it twice: a small unmeasured warmup (to settle pipeline state of any
483	 * piece we missed -- branch predictor, vector unit first-use), then the
484	 * PFM-bracketed measured call. */
485	extern void extracted_kernel(long iters);
486	
487	int main(void)
488	{{
489	    /* warmup: small fixed count, NOT bracketed by PFM */
490	    extracted_kernel({warmup_iters});
491	
492	    /* measure: PFM brackets exactly the {iters} measured iterations */
493	    startPFM
494	    extracted_kernel({iters});
495	    stopPFM
496	
497	    readResult();
498	
499	    /* Report meta so post-processing can compute cycles/iter */
500	    printf("Inner-loop iterations per call: {iters}\\n");
501	    printf("Inner-loop body instructions:   {body_count}\\n");
502	
503	    /* run_fpga_test.sh greps for this -- harness has no real correctness
504	     * check (we're measuring synthetic data), but the line is required. */
505	    printf("accuracy checking pass\\n");
506	    return 0;
507	}}
508	'''
509	
510	
511	def emit_fpga(analysis, iters, bufsize, warmup_iters=5):
512	    live_ins = analysis["live_in_regs"]
513	    counter = pick_counter_reg(live_ins)
514	    loop_start_pc = int(analysis["loop_start_pc"], 16)
515	    loop_end_pc = int(analysis["loop_end_pc"], 16)
516	
517	    init_lines = emit_live_in_init(live_ins)
518	    reset_lines = emit_live_in_reset(live_ins)
519	    body_lines = emit_loop_body(analysis["loop_body_lines"],
520	                                loop_start_pc, loop_end_pc)
521	
522	    callee = callee_saved_clobbered(live_ins, counter)
523	    # 8 bytes for ra + 8 bytes per callee-saved reg, rounded up to 16-byte
524	    # alignment.
525	    frame_size = 8 * (1 + len(callee))
526	    frame_size = (frame_size + 15) & ~15
527	
528	    save_lines = []
529	    restore_lines = []
530	    for i, r in enumerate(callee, start=1):
531	        save_lines.append(f"    sd {r}, {i*8}(sp)")
532	        restore_lines.append(f"    ld {r}, {i*8}(sp)")
533	    save_block = "\n".join(save_lines)
534	    restore_block = "\n".join(restore_lines)
535	
536	    indent = lambda lines: "\n".join(lines)
537	
538	    header = FPGA_KERNEL_HEADER.format(
539	        fn=analysis["containing_fn"],
540	        hot_pc=analysis["hot_block_pc"],
541	        ecount=analysis["hot_block_ecount"],
542	        loop_start=analysis["loop_start_pc"],
543	        loop_end=analysis["loop_end_pc"],
544	        body_count=analysis["loop_body_count"],
545	        n_scalar=len(live_ins["scalar"]),
546	        n_vector=len(live_ins["vector"]),
547	        bufsize=bufsize,
548	        counter=counter,
549	        iters=iters,
550	        est_insns=analysis["loop_body_count"] * iters,
551	        frame_size=frame_size,
552	        save_block=save_block,
553	    )
554	    body_section = FPGA_KERNEL_BODY.format(
555	        init=indent(init_lines),
556	        counter=counter,
557	        iters=iters,
558	        reset=indent(reset_lines),
559	        body=indent(body_lines),
560	        restore_block=restore_block,
561	        frame_size=frame_size,
562	    )
563	    asm_text = header + "\n" + body_section
564	
565	    wrapper_text = FPGA_WRAPPER_C.format(
566	        fn=analysis["containing_fn"],
567	        iters=iters,
568	        warmup_iters=warmup_iters,
569	        body_count=analysis["loop_body_count"],
570	    )
571	    return asm_text, wrapper_text
572	
573	
574	# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
575	# CLI
576	# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
577	
578	def main():
579	    ap = argparse.ArgumentParser()
580	    ap.add_argument("--analysis", required=True, type=Path)
581	    ap.add_argument("--mode",     choices=("standalone", "fpga"),
582	                    default="standalone")
583	    ap.add_argument("--output",   required=True, type=Path,
584	                    help="Output .s path (asm). In fpga mode, the C wrapper "
585	                         "goes to <output stem>.c.")
586	    ap.add_argument("--output-c", type=Path,
587	                    help="Override C wrapper path (fpga mode only).")
588	    ap.add_argument("--warmup",   type=int, default=5,
589	                    help="Standalone-mode warmup iters (ignored in fpga mode).")
590	    ap.add_argument("--fpga-warmup-iters", type=int, default=5,
591	                    help="FPGA mode only: warmup iter count passed to the "
592	                         "first (unmeasured) call to extracted_kernel. "
593	                         "5 is enough to settle a 5-stage in-order pipeline "
594	                         "and branch predictor. Set to 0 to skip warmup.")
595	    ap.add_argument("--iters",    type=int, default=100)
596	    ap.add_argument("--buf-size", type=int, default=65536)
597	    args = ap.parse_args()
598	
599	    analysis = json.loads(args.analysis.read_text())
600	
601	    if args.mode == "standalone":
602	        text = emit_standalone(analysis, args.warmup, args.iters, args.buf_size)
603	        args.output.write_text(text)
604	        print(f"[ok] standalone harness -> {args.output} "
605	              f"({args.iters} measured iters, {args.warmup} warmup)")
606	    else:
607	        asm, c = emit_fpga(analysis, args.iters, args.buf_size,
608	                           warmup_iters=args.fpga_warmup_iters)
609	        out_c = args.output_c or args.output.with_suffix(".c")
610	        args.output.write_text(asm)
611	        out_c.write_text(c)
612	        print(f"[ok] fpga kernel  -> {args.output}")
613	        print(f"[ok] fpga wrapper -> {out_c}")
614	
615	
616	if __name__ == "__main__":
617	    main()
618
